fa: The world's largest hot desert, roughly the size of the United States; was a lush savanna with lakes and hippos just 6,000 years ago before a shift in Earth's axial tilt dried it out.
Mostly covered in grass and scrub rather than bare sand; home to the San people, who have lived as hunter-gatherers here for over 20,000 years.
Considered the world's oldest desert at over 55 million years; its red dunes at Sossusvlei, among the tallest on Earth, meet the cold Atlantic fog that sustains desert-adapted wildlife.
Named for the whale bones and shipwrecks littering its shore; dense Atlantic fog has wrecked over a thousand ships against its dunes since the 15th century.
One of the hottest and lowest places on Earth; home to Erta Ale's lava lake and vast turquoise-and-yellow sulfur fields at Dallol.
A flat, cracked-clay desert near Lake Turkana, close to where some of humanity's oldest fossil ancestors have been unearthed.
Home to over 200 ancient pyramids built by the Kingdom of Kush — more than in all of Egypt, though far less visited.
One of the most barren parts of the Sahara; the Libyan Desert Glass found here, formed by an ancient meteor airburst, was used in a brooch found in Tutankhamun's tomb.
Covers two-thirds of Egypt; legend holds that an entire Persian army of 50,000 men vanished into its sands in 525 BC, never to be found.
Wind-carved chalk-white rock formations rise like mushrooms and icebergs from the sand, the eroded remains of an ancient seabed.
According to tradition, Moses received the Ten Commandments atop Mount Sinai; its granite peaks rise dramatically straight from the desert floor.

co: Saudi Arabia and the Arabian Peninsula
fa: Covers most of the Arabian Peninsula; the world's largest conventional oil reserves were discovered beneath its sands in the 20th century.
Known as the 'Empty Quarter', the largest continuous sand sea on Earth, with dunes up to 250 m tall; explorer Wilfred Thesiger crossed it by camel in the 1940s.
Crossed for millennia by camel caravans linking Mesopotamia to the Mediterranean; the ancient oasis city of Palmyra grew wealthy controlling its trade routes.
Covers over half of Israel; ancient Nabatean engineers built sophisticated water-channeling systems here 2,000 years ago that are still studied today.
The Dead Sea Scrolls, the oldest known biblical manuscripts, were discovered hidden in caves above the Dead Sea here in 1947.
Nicknamed the 'Valley of the Moon'; T.E. Lawrence based his WWI operations here, and its red sandstone canyons have stood in for Mars in several films.
Holds the record for the hottest land surface temperature ever measured by satellite — 70.7°C — and contains the towering Lut yardangs, among the tallest sand formations on Earth.
A vast salt desert where a thin, deceptively solid-looking crust can collapse into deep mud beneath unwary travelers.
Its name literally means 'place of sand'; shifting dunes here have buried and revealed ancient caravan routes for centuries.
Home to the 'Door to Hell', a natural gas crater accidentally ignited by Soviet engineers in 1971 that has burned continuously ever since.
Its name means 'Red Sand'; the shrinking Aral Sea on its edge has left rusting fishing boats stranded kilometers from any water.
One of the few deserts cold enough for snow; the first-ever dinosaur eggs and nest fossils were discovered here in the 1920s.
Its name is often translated as 'place of no return'; Bronze Age travelers with European features, mummified by the arid climate, were found along its ancient Silk Road routes.
A rapidly expanding desert on the Mongolian Plateau where China has planted a 'Great Green Wall' of trees to halt the encroaching sand.
Home to the last truly wild population of Przewalski's horse before 20th-century reintroduction efforts, in one of Central Asia's most isolated basins.
Contains 'singing dunes' that produce a deep booming sound as sand cascades down their faces — an effect studied by acousticians for over a century.
The most densely populated desert on Earth, with over 80 people per km²; its dunes host the Jaisalmer 'Golden City', built from local sandstone.
North America's largest desert, defined by dozens of north-south mountain ranges separated by valleys that trap water with no outlet to the sea.
North America's largest hot desert; its gypsum dunes at White Sands, New Mexico are the largest gypsum dune field on the planet.
The only place on Earth where the saguaro cactus grows wild, some living over 150 years and reaching 12 meters tall.
Home to Death Valley, the hottest and driest place in North America, where ground temperatures have been recorded above 90°C.
Its banded cliffs of red, orange and lavender mudstone, visible across the Petrified Forest, were laid down over 200 million years ago.
A vast, perfectly flat ancient lakebed that hosts the Burning Man festival each year and several land-speed world records.
The driest non-polar desert on Earth; some weather stations have never recorded a drop of rain, and its clear skies host the world's most powerful astronomical telescopes.
South America's largest desert, shaped by the Andes blocking Pacific moisture; ferocious year-round winds can exceed 100 km/h.
A little-known desert ecoregion rich in cacti and shrubland, stretching along Argentina's western edge in the rain shadow of the Andes.
A foggy coastal desert kept rain-free by the cold Humboldt Current; the El Niño phenomenon occasionally floods it, briefly turning sand into wildflower fields.
A wind-battered desert where dunes meet the Caribbean Sea; home to the Wayuu, Colombia's largest Indigenous group, who still travel by donkey cart.
Australia's largest desert; its parallel sand dunes, some over 30 km long, were shaped over millions of years by steady prevailing winds.
Crossed by Australia's longest fence-line route, the Canning Stock Route, originally built to drive cattle nearly 1,800 km to market.
Contains over 1,100 parallel red sand dunes running for hundreds of kilometers, among the longest dune systems on Earth.
So remote that its only road, the Tanami Track, was built in the 1940s to reach a short-lived gold rush in the middle of nowhere.
Named after explorer Alfred Gibson, who died of thirst attempting to cross it in 1874 — one of the last major Australian deserts to be mapped by Europeans.
Crossed by the legendary Birdsville Track; explorers Burke and Wills perished near its edge in 1861 after a doomed attempt to cross the continent.
Thousands of jagged limestone spires up to 3.5 m tall rise from yellow sand, formed from ancient seashells over the past 500,000 years.
The largest desert on Earth by area — bigger than the Sahara — because deserts are defined by low precipitation, not heat; over 70% of the planet's fresh water is locked in its ice.
Despite being covered in ice and snow, the polar air holds so little moisture that some interior valleys receive less precipitation each year than the Sahara.
